subject: PATIENT
heading: Hospital Course:__page_no__2
text: hospital course: patient is a 59 y.o. year old female with a past medical history of esrd 2/2 hypertensive nephropathy and diabetic nephropathy 2/2 ddkt on [encounter_date] with [provider_name], presenting to the ed today with anuria, nausea, and vomiting. post-transplant course complicated by recurrent uti, e-coli bacteremia, c.diff infection from [encounter_date]- [encounter_date] and wound infection requiring vac placement (removed [encounter_date]). she was readmitted from [encounter_date]- [encounter_date] for aki, severe hyperglycemia (bg 662), and e.coli bacteremia and metapneumovirus. she was discharged to home on po vanc due to her prior c.diff infection and vantin to cover e.coli, both courses of which she finished.

text: hospital course: patient is a 59 y.o. year old female with a past medical history of esrd 2/2 hypertensive nephropathy and diabetic nephropathy 2/2 ddkt on [encounter_date] with [provider_name], presenting to the ed today with anuria, nausea, and vomiting. post-transplant course complicated by recurrent uti, e-coli bacteremia, c.diff infection from [encounter_date]- [encounter_date] and wound infection requiring vac placement (removed [encounter_date]). she was readmitted from [encounter_date]- [encounter_date] for aki, severe hyperglycemia (bg 662), and e.coli bacteremia and metapneumovirus. she was discharged to home on po vanc due to her prior c.diff infection and vantin to cover e.coli, both courses of which she finished.

subject: PATIENT
heading: Resolved Hospital Problems__page_no__2
text: resolved hospital problems patient represented to [facility_name] ed on [encounter_date] for 4 day history of n/v, fatigue, constipation, fevers (t-max 101f). comprehensive infectious workup was performed with negative cultures, however urine culture from [encounter_date] grew > 100k multidrug resistant e-coli. patient also reported oliguria on admission with scr of 1.4 which has improved to scr of 0.9 and continues to make urine. since admission, patient has been receiving ceftriaxone with improvement in symptoms. patient will be discharged on 10 day course of keflex and oral vanco for c-diff prophylaxis, given her prior history of c-diff. constipation has also resolved and patient has been having regular bowel movements. of note, patient was hypocalcemic with ica of 0.64 for which patient received iv and po calcium supplementation and will be discharged on os-cal tabs. since admission, patient remained hemodynamically stable. transplant nephrology was consulted on admission and continued to follow patient throughout this hospitalization. renal allograft function and electrolytes were monitored closely, and the latter were replaced as necessary. maintenance immunosuppression, transplant prophylaxis, and home medications were resumed accordingly. today she is stable for hospital discharge to home with close outpatient follow up. kidney allograft function is improving. uop 1650 ml over last 24 hours, with scr 0.9 on day of discharge. pain is well controlled on oral pain medications. patient is tolerating a solid diabetic diet, has regular bowel function, and is ambulating without assistance. she will need follow up at the transplant institute on [encounter_date] with labs to be drawn earlier that morning prior to the clinic appointment. spoke with patient at bedside on day of discharge, who was updated on hospitalization/clinical course, plan for discharge today, discharge medication changes (as will be listed in avs and on medactionplan), and subsequent follow-up plan and instructions (as detailed above).

performed with negative cultures, however urine culture from [encounter_date] grew > 100k multidrug resistant e-coli. patient also reported oliguria on admission with scr of 1.4 which has improved to scr of 0.9 and continues to make urine. since admission, patient has been receiving ceftriaxone with improvement in symptoms. patient will be discharged on 10 day course of keflex and oral vanco for c-diff prophylaxis, given her prior history of c-diff. constipation has also resolved and patient has been having regular bowel movements. of note, patient was hypocalcemic with ica of 0.64 for which patient received iv and po calcium supplementation and will be discharged on os-cal tabs. since admission, patient remained hemodynamically stable. transplant nephrology was consulted on admission and continued to follow patient throughout this hospitalization. renal allograft function and electrolytes were monitored closely, and the latter were replaced as necessary. maintenance immunosuppression, transplant prophylaxis, and home medications were resumed accordingly. today she is stable for hospital discharge to home with close outpatient follow up. kidney allograft function is improving. uop 1650 ml over last 24 hours, with scr 0.9 on day of discharge. pain is well controlled on oral pain medications. patient is tolerating a solid diabetic diet, has regular bowel function, and is ambulating without assistance. she will need follow up at the transplant institute on [encounter_date] with labs to be drawn earlier that morning prior to the clinic appointment. spoke with patient at bedside on day of discharge, who was updated on hospitalization/clinical course, plan for discharge today, discharge medication changes (as will be listed in avs and on medactionplan), and subsequent follow-up plan and instructions (as detailed above).

subject: PATIENT
heading: Patient Instructions:__page_no__7
text: patient instructions: - take your medications as prescribed. - may shower, no soaking in tubs/pools. - keep wound clean and dry. - monitor for signs of incisional/wound infection, such as redness, swelling, warmth, increased tenderness, or new oozing/drainage of surrounding staples/incision/drain site. - no lifting or pushing over 10 lbs for 3 months to prevent abdominal incision hernias. - no driving for three weeks after surgery or while taking narcotics. - you should walk as much as you can after your transplant. then, start incorporating more physical activity into your daily life, including participating in at least 30 minutes of moderate exercise five days a week. - no sexual activity until cleared by physician in clinic. - regular diet, no concentrated sweets - please stay hydrated by drinking at least 2 l of water and other fluids each day. - avoid eating fast food, processed food, and foods high in unhealthy fats such as saturated fats in butter and red meat. - follow food safety practices to reduce risk of infection. - limit your salt and sodium intake. - limit your caffeine intake and avoid alcohol. - avoid grapefruit and grapefruit juice, pomegranate and seville oranges due to their effects on a group of immunosuppressive medications (prograf). - do not take nsaids, such as ibuprofen, naproxen, etc. (unless directed by your physician). - check your blood sugar 4 times daily before meals and at bedtime. - please record the following and bring to clinic: fluid intake, urine output, drain output, blood sugar, and blood pressure. - return to the emergency department if you develop fevers over 100.4 f, increasing incisional pain, redness, swelling, discharge, marked decrease in urine output, inability to tolerate food or drink, or overall worsening condition. - for non-urgent concerns, please call the transplant institute. main line: [phone] post kidney triage line: [phone] x 33748 after hours line: [phone]

subject: PATIENT
heading: Patient Instructions:__page_no__7
text: patient instructions: - take your medications as prescribed. - may shower, no soaking in tubs/pools. - keep wound clean and dry. - monitor for signs of incisional/wound infection, such as redness, swelling, warmth, increased tenderness, or new oozing/drainage of surrounding staples/incision/drain site. - no lifting or pushing over 10 lbs for 3 months to prevent abdominal incision hernias. - no driving for three weeks after surgery or while taking narcotics. - you should walk as much as you can after your transplant. then, start incorporating more physical activity into your daily life, including participating in at least 30 minutes of moderate exercise five days a week. - no sexual activity until cleared by physician in clinic. - regular diet, no concentrated sweets - please stay hydrated by drinking at least 2 l of water and other fluids each day. - avoid eating fast food, processed food, and foods high in unhealthy fats such as saturated fats in butter and red meat. - follow food safety practices to reduce risk of infection. - limit your salt and sodium intake. - limit your caffeine intake and avoid alcohol. - avoid grapefruit and grapefruit juice, pomegranate and seville oranges due to their effects on a group of immunosuppressive medications (prograf). - do not take nsaids, such as ibuprofen, naproxen, etc. (unless directed by your physician). - check your blood sugar 4 times daily before meals and at bedtime. - please record the following and bring to clinic: fluid intake, urine output, drain output, blood sugar, and blood pressure. - return to the emergency department if you develop fevers over 100.4 f, increasing incisional pain, redness, swelling, discharge, marked decrease in urine output, inability to tolerate food or drink, or overall worsening condition. - for non-urgent concerns, please call the transplant institute. main line: [phone] post kidney triage line: [phone] x 33748 after hours line: [phone]

text: patient instructions: - take your medications as prescribed. - may shower, no soaking in tubs/pools. - keep wound clean and dry. - monitor for signs of incisional/wound infection, such as redness, swelling, warmth, increased tenderness, or new oozing/drainage of surrounding staples/incision/drain site. - no lifting or pushing over 10 lbs for 3 months to prevent abdominal incision hernias. - no driving for three weeks after surgery or while taking narcotics. - you should walk as much as you can after your transplant. then, start incorporating more physical activity into your daily life, including participating in at least 30 minutes of moderate exercise five days a week. - no sexual activity until cleared by physician in clinic. - regular diet, no concentrated sweets - please stay hydrated by drinking at least 2 l of water and other fluids each day. - avoid eating fast food, processed food, and foods high in unhealthy fats such as saturated fats in butter and red meat. - follow food safety practices to reduce risk of infection. - limit your salt and sodium intake. - limit your caffeine intake and avoid alcohol. - avoid grapefruit and grapefruit juice, pomegranate and seville oranges due to their effects on a group of immunosuppressive medications (prograf). - do not take nsaids, such as ibuprofen, naproxen, etc. (unless directed by your physician). - check your blood sugar 4 times daily before meals and at bedtime. - please record the following and bring to clinic: fluid intake, urine output, drain output, blood sugar, and blood pressure. - return to the emergency department if you develop fevers over 100.4 f, increasing incisional pain, redness, swelling, discharge, marked decrease in urine output, inability to tolerate food or drink, or overall worsening condition. - for non-urgent concerns, please call the transplant institute. main line: [phone] post kidney triage line: [phone] x 33748 after hours line: [phone]

text: patient instructions: - take your medications as prescribed. - may shower, no soaking in tubs/pools. - keep wound clean and dry. - monitor for signs of incisional/wound infection, such as redness, swelling, warmth, increased tenderness, or new oozing/drainage of surrounding staples/incision/drain site. - no lifting or pushing over 10 lbs for 3 months to prevent abdominal incision hernias. - no driving for three weeks after surgery or while taking narcotics. - you should walk as much as you can after your transplant. then, start incorporating more physical activity into your daily life, including participating in at least 30 minutes of moderate exercise five days a week. - no sexual activity until cleared by physician in clinic. - regular diet, no concentrated sweets - please stay hydrated by drinking at least 2 l of water and other fluids each day. - avoid eating fast food, processed food, and foods high in unhealthy fats such as saturated fats in butter and red meat. - follow food safety practices to reduce risk of infection. - limit your salt and sodium intake. - limit your caffeine intake and avoid alcohol. - avoid grapefruit and grapefruit juice, pomegranate and seville oranges due to their effects on a group of immunosuppressive medications (prograf). - do not take nsaids, such as ibuprofen, naproxen, etc. (unless directed by your physician). - check your blood sugar 4 times daily before meals and at bedtime. - please record the following and bring to clinic: fluid intake, urine output, drain output, blood sugar, and blood pressure. - return to the emergency department if you develop fevers over 100.4 f, increasing incisional pain, redness, swelling, discharge, marked decrease in urine output, inability to tolerate food or drink, or overall worsening condition. - for non-urgent concerns, please call the transplant institute. main line: [phone] post kidney triage line: [phone] x 33748 after hours line: [phone]

heading: Kidney Transplant History & Physical__page_no__9
text: kidney transplant history & physical admission date and time: [encounter_date] 10:53 am attending provider: [provider_name]

subject: PATIENT
heading: CHIEF COMPLAINT__page_no__9
text: chief complaint fever, oliguria/anuria, nausea, vomiting hpi: patient is a 59 y.o. year old female with a past medical history of esrd 2/2 hypertensive nephropathy and diabetic nephropathy 2/2 ddkt on [encounter_date] with [provider_name], presenting to the ed today with anuria, nausea, and vomiting. post-transplant course complicated by c.diff infection from [encounter_date] and wound infection requiring vac placement (removed [encounter_date]). she was readmitted from [encounter_date] for aki, severe hyperglycemia (bg 662), and e.coli bacteremia and metapneumovirus. she was discharged to home on po vanc due to her prior c.diff infection and vantin to cover e.coli, both courses of which she finished. patient currently presents with 4 days of oliguria, nausea, vomiting, and subjective fevers at home. she states she was unable to keep anything down except tylenol, and even water can trigger her nausea. she had only urinated "a small amount" over the first 3 days and did not urinate at all over the past 24 hours. she also endorses flu-like symptoms and notes her granddaughter had recently been sick. she denies chest pain, palpitations, sob, cough, abdominal pain, constipation, or diarrhea. she denies dysuria or hematuria.

text: chief complaint fever, oliguria/anuria, nausea, vomiting hpi: patient is a 59 y.o. year old female with a past medical history of esrd 2/2 hypertensive nephropathy and diabetic nephropathy 2/2 ddkt on [encounter_date] with [provider_name], presenting to the ed today with anuria, nausea, and vomiting. post-transplant course complicated by c.diff infection from [encounter_date] and wound infection requiring vac placement (removed [encounter_date]). she was readmitted from [encounter_date] for aki, severe hyperglycemia (bg 662), and e.coli bacteremia and metapneumovirus. she was discharged to home on po vanc due to her prior c.diff infection and vantin to cover e.coli, both courses of which she finished. patient currently presents with 4 days of oliguria, nausea, vomiting, and subjective fevers at home. she states she was unable to keep anything down except tylenol, and even water can trigger her nausea. she had only urinated "a small amount" over the first 3 days and did not urinate at all over the past 24 hours. she also endorses flu-like symptoms and notes her granddaughter had recently been sick. she denies chest pain, palpitations, sob, cough, abdominal pain, constipation, or diarrhea. she denies dysuria or hematuria.

text: chief complaint fever, oliguria/anuria, nausea, vomiting hpi: patient is a 59 y.o. year old female with a past medical history of esrd 2/2 hypertensive nephropathy and diabetic nephropathy 2/2 ddkt on [encounter_date] with [provider_name], presenting to the ed today with anuria, nausea, and vomiting. post-transplant course complicated by c.diff infection from [encounter_date] and wound infection requiring vac placement (removed [encounter_date]). she was readmitted from [encounter_date] for aki, severe hyperglycemia (bg 662), and e.coli bacteremia and metapneumovirus. she was discharged to home on po vanc due to her prior c.diff infection and vantin to cover e.coli, both courses of which she finished. patient currently presents with 4 days of oliguria, nausea, vomiting, and subjective fevers at home. she states she was unable to keep anything down except tylenol, and even water can trigger her nausea. she had only urinated "a small amount" over the first 3 days and did not urinate at all over the past 24 hours. she also endorses flu-like symptoms and notes her granddaughter had recently been sick. she denies chest pain, palpitations, sob, cough, abdominal pain, constipation, or diarrhea. she denies dysuria or hematuria.

text: chief complaint fever, oliguria/anuria, nausea, vomiting hpi: patient is a 59 y.o. year old female with a past medical history of esrd 2/2 hypertensive nephropathy and diabetic nephropathy 2/2 ddkt on [encounter_date] with [provider_name], presenting to the ed today with anuria, nausea, and vomiting. post-transplant course complicated by c.diff infection from [encounter_date] and wound infection requiring vac placement (removed [encounter_date]). she was readmitted from [encounter_date] for aki, severe hyperglycemia (bg 662), and e.coli bacteremia and metapneumovirus. she was discharged to home on po vanc due to her prior c.diff infection and vantin to cover e.coli, both courses of which she finished. patient currently presents with 4 days of oliguria, nausea, vomiting, and subjective fevers at home. she states she was unable to keep anything down except tylenol, and even water can trigger her nausea. she had only urinated "a small amount" over the first 3 days and did not urinate at all over the past 24 hours. she also endorses flu-like symptoms and notes her granddaughter had recently been sick. she denies chest pain, palpitations, sob, cough, abdominal pain, constipation, or diarrhea. she denies dysuria or hematuria. currently taking prograf 1 mg 1 and 1 bid, prednisone 5 mg qd, and cellcept 500 mg bid. for infection prophylaxis she takes bactrim 800-160 mwf and valcyte 450mg qd. she states she has been compliant with all of her medications but missed them during the past 4 days due to vomiting them up.

text: chief complaint fever, oliguria/anuria, nausea, vomiting hpi: patient is a 59 y.o. year old female with a past medical history of esrd 2/2 hypertensive nephropathy and diabetic nephropathy 2/2 ddkt on [encounter_date] with [provider_name], presenting to the ed today with anuria, nausea, and vomiting. post-transplant course complicated by c.diff infection from [encounter_date] and wound infection requiring vac placement (removed [encounter_date]). she was readmitted from [encounter_date] for aki, severe hyperglycemia (bg 662), and e.coli bacteremia and metapneumovirus. she was discharged to home on po vanc due to her prior c.diff infection and vantin to cover e.coli, both courses of which she finished. patient currently presents with 4 days of oliguria, nausea, vomiting, and subjective fevers at home. she states she was unable to keep anything down except tylenol, and even water can trigger her nausea. she had only urinated "a small amount" over the first 3 days and did not urinate at all over the past 24 hours. she also endorses flu-like symptoms and notes her granddaughter had recently been sick. she denies chest pain, palpitations, sob, cough, abdominal pain, constipation, or diarrhea. she denies dysuria or hematuria. currently taking prograf 1 mg 1 and 1 bid, prednisone 5 mg qd, and cellcept 500 mg bid. for infection prophylaxis she takes bactrim 800-160 mwf and valcyte 450mg qd. she states she has been compliant with all of her medications but missed them during the past 4 days due to vomiting them up.

entity: ct abdomen and pelvis wo con
subject: PATIENT
heading: PHYSICAL EXAM__page_no__14
text: ct abdomen and pelvis wo con (74176)[encounter_date] 4:07 pm history:[age] years old female being evaluated for sepsis, history of kidney transplant comparison: [encounter_date] procedure: helical ct images were obtained of the abdomen and pelvis without intravenous contrast. sagittal and coronal reconstructions are provided. oral contrast: none. additional images / reformats: none total radiation dose: ctdivol: 15 mgy dlp: 860 mgy x cm findings: lower chest: mild coronary artery calcifications are noted. scattered calcified granuloma. bibasilar atelectasis. liver: normal. gallbladder and biliary tree: no calcified gallstones. normal caliber wall. no intra- or extrahepatic biliary ductal dilation. pancreas: normal. spleen: similar-appearing subcentimeter hypodense splenic lesion. the spleen is slightly enlarged measuring 14 cm. bowel: scattered diverticulosis with no evidence of diverticulitis. postsurgical changes related to appendectomy is noted. adrenals: normal. kidneys and ureter: there is similar-appearing atrophy of bilateral native kidneys with similar-appearing nonobstructive renal calculi. there is a right lower quadrant renal transplant with no hydronephrosis or peritransplant fluid collection. bladder: normal. reproductive organs: normal. lymph nodes:no lymphadenopathy. peritoneum: no ascites or free air. no other fluid collection. vessels: mild atherosclerotic calcifications of the abdominal aorta and branch vessels are noted. retroperitoneum: normal. abdominal wall: unchanged soft tissue thickening along the surgical incision in the right anterolateral abdominal wall. scattered soft tissue nodule seen within the abdominal wall, which may be related to injections. bones: scattered degenerative changes of the osseous structures are noted. impression: right lower quadrant transplant kidney with no evidence of hydronephrosis or peritransplant fluid collection. diverticulosis with no evidence of diverticulitis.

text: ct abdomen and pelvis wo con (74176)[encounter_date] 4:07 pm history:[age] years old female being evaluated for sepsis, history of kidney transplant comparison: [encounter_date] procedure: helical ct images were obtained of the abdomen and pelvis without intravenous contrast. sagittal and coronal reconstructions are provided. oral contrast: none. additional images / reformats: none total radiation dose: ctdivol: 15 mgy dlp: 860 mgy x cm findings: lower chest: mild coronary artery calcifications are noted. scattered calcified granuloma. bibasilar atelectasis. liver: normal. gallbladder and biliary tree: no calcified gallstones. normal caliber wall. no intra- or extrahepatic biliary ductal dilation. pancreas: normal. spleen: similar-appearing subcentimeter hypodense splenic lesion. the spleen is slightly enlarged measuring 14 cm. bowel: scattered diverticulosis with no evidence of diverticulitis. postsurgical changes related to appendectomy is noted. adrenals: normal. kidneys and ureter: there is similar-appearing atrophy of bilateral native kidneys with similar-appearing nonobstructive renal calculi. there is a right lower quadrant renal transplant with no hydronephrosis or peritransplant fluid collection. bladder: normal. reproductive organs: normal. lymph nodes:no lymphadenopathy. peritoneum: no ascites or free air. no other fluid collection. vessels: mild atherosclerotic calcifications of the abdominal aorta and branch vessels are noted. retroperitoneum: normal. abdominal wall: unchanged soft tissue thickening along the surgical incision in the right anterolateral abdominal wall. scattered soft tissue nodule seen within the abdominal wall, which may be related to injections. bones: scattered degenerative changes of the osseous structures are noted. impression: right lower quadrant transplant kidney with no evidence of hydronephrosis or peritransplant fluid collection. diverticulosis with no evidence of diverticulitis.

text: result date: [encounter_date] diffuse cortical cutback. bladder: normal impression: elevated peak systolic velocity of the main renal artery at the anastomosis and midportion, which may suggest renal artery stenosis. diffuse cortical cutback of the perfusion, may be related to the above-described renal artery stenosis and/or parenchymal dysfunction. dictated: [provider_name] reviewed images personally and agree with interpretation, as currently edited. electronically signed / radiologist [provider_name] on [encounter_date] 3:47 pm assessment: patient is a 59 y.o. year old female with past medical history of esrd 2/2 hypertensive nephropathy and diabetic nephropathy 2/2 ddkt on [encounter_date] with [provider_name], with post-recovery course c/b c.diff infection and wound infection ([encounter_date]) and recent admission for aki, dka, and e.coli bacteremia and metapneumovirus, presenting with anuria/oliguria, nausea, vomiting, and fever and missed medication doses due to vomiting. the patient's low uop is most likely due to dehydration from gastroenteritis. at this time we cannot exclude cmv as a potential cause of her symptoms. she also presents with hyperglycemia and acidosis requiring correction. renal artery stenosis was also detected on recent ultrasound which is likely not contributing to her current presentation.

subject: PATIENT
heading: PLAN:__page_no__16
text: plan: - admit to transplant service - discontinue ceftriaxone - check ua and ucx - initiate sodium bicarb gtt at 100 cc/hr, keep on until tomorrow am pending repeat cmp - initiate home insulin: - lantus 25u qhs - humalog 6u ac - ssi with accuchecks and hypoglycemia protocol achs check cmv labs - cmv - ebv - bk - zofran or nausea - ok to stop trending lactate - repeat cmp at midnight - strict i&o daily cbc with differential, bmp, mag, phos, fk506 - immunosuppression: - hold cellcept 500 mg bid - continue prograf 1 mg 1 and 1 bii - continue prednisone 5 mg qd - prophylaxis: prophylaxis continue bactrim 800-160 mwf continue valcyte 450mg qd. - resume other home meds: - coreg 25mg bid - sodium bicarbonate 650mg bid - holding mag-ox due to nausea - diabetic diet with restricted potassium, no concentrated sweets patient was discussed with senior fellow [provider_name] and attending [provider_name], with whom the plan was formulated.

text: plan: - admit to transplant service - discontinue ceftriaxone - check ua and ucx - initiate sodium bicarb gtt at 100 cc/hr, keep on until tomorrow am pending repeat cmp - initiate home insulin: - lantus 25u qhs - humalog 6u ac - ssi with accuchecks and hypoglycemia protocol achs check cmv labs - cmv - ebv - bk - zofran or nausea - ok to stop trending lactate - repeat cmp at midnight - strict i&o daily cbc with differential, bmp, mag, phos, fk506 - immunosuppression: - hold cellcept 500 mg bid - continue prograf 1 mg 1 and 1 bii - continue prednisone 5 mg qd - prophylaxis: prophylaxis continue bactrim 800-160 mwf continue valcyte 450mg qd. - resume other home meds: - coreg 25mg bid - sodium bicarbonate 650mg bid - holding mag-ox due to nausea - diabetic diet with restricted potassium, no concentrated sweets patient was discussed with senior fellow [provider_name] and attending [provider_name], with whom the plan was formulated.
